evals.name: new-feature-end-to-end
evals.prompt: Brainstorm adding real-time collaboration to our document editor. Multiple users should be able to edit the same document simultaneously, like Google Docs.
evals.expected_output: A full brainstorm covering questioning, non-functional requirements, edge cases, dependency surfacing, YAGNI, self-review, and a tight design document
expectations: Asks the right questions: clarifies purpose, scope, success criteria, and user/system constraints before proposing solutions
Does not miss important questions: covers concurrency model (CRDT vs OT), presence/cursor behavior, offline editing, conflict resolution policy, and persistence boundary
Uses the AskUserQuestion tool for every question instead of embedding questions in plain text
Identifies non-functional requirements explicitly: performance/latency target, scalability bound, reliability, security/auth, observability
Hunts for edge cases: concurrent edits on the same range, network partition, stale clients, document deleted mid-edit, abusive payload sizes
Surfaces external dependencies (CRDT lib, WebSocket service) with maturity signals, auth surface, env var names, and an ordered fallback chain ending in a paid API or build-our-own option
Stress-tests the chosen approach against multiple lenses (end user, maintainer, scale, adjacent systems, security, failure modes)
Applies YAGNI: every knob, flag, and optional feature is challenged; defaults to hardcoding unless the value is genuinely empirical
Runs a self-review / spec-reviewer pass on the written design before handoff
Pauses for user approval on the written design before flowing to the next stage
Produces a design document at docs/spec/<SPEC_NAME>/design.md with EARS-style requirement IDs (F1, NF1, etc.)
anti_expectations: Does not write any code, scaffolding, or pseudocode during the brainstorm
Does not embed function bodies or implementation code blocks in the design document — only interfaces, signatures, and data shapes
Does not repeat the same conclusion across Key Insights, Architectural Decisions, and Risks sections
Does not pile multiple paragraphs of justification on already-settled decisions
Does not include tautological assumptions ('UUIDs are unique') or 'we'll see how it goes' placeholders
Does not include straw-man alternative approaches with full Pros/Cons when only one approach is real
Does not skip to a solution before exploring the problem space
Does not proceed to implementation before explicit user approval

evals.name: minor-change-stays-proportionate
evals.prompt: Think through adding a dark mode toggle to our settings page. We already have a theme context provider, just need the UI toggle and to persist the preference.
evals.expected_output: A brainstorm scaled to a minor change: short questioning, focused exploration, brief design document, sections omitted when empty
expectations: Scales depth to the problem: short questioning round, brief exploration, concise design

Asks the right questions even on a small change: persistence target (localStorage vs server), SSR/flash-of-wrong-theme behavior, system-preference detection, accessibility (prefers-reduced-motion contrast)
Identifies at least one non-functional concern (SSR compatibility, accessibility, persistence semantics)
Identifies edge cases proportionate to scope (system preference change while app is open, stored preference for a no-longer-supported theme, flash on initial load)
Uses the AskUserQuestion tool for questions
Applies YAGNI: rejects unnecessary knobs (custom theme builder, per-page overrides, animation timing config) for a binary toggle
Omits sections that have no genuine content (no Assumptions section if nothing is assumed; no Open Questions section if none block planning)
Produces a brief design document at docs/spec/<SPEC_NAME>/design.md
anti_expectations: Does not skip brainstorming entirely on the grounds that the problem is simple
Does not jump to writing implementation code or providing code snippets
Does not produce an exhaustive multi-page design disproportionate to the problem
Does not invent multiple straw-man alternative approaches when only one is realistic
Does not repeat the same insight across multiple sections in the design document

evals.name: structural-refactoring
evals.prompt: Help me think through refactoring our payment processing module. It's a 2000-line file handling Stripe, PayPal, and bank transfers all in one class. Error handling is inconsistent and adding new payment methods is painful.
evals.expected_output: Exploration that reads the existing code, identifies boundaries and contracts, presents alternative refactor shapes, and produces an architectural design without code
expectations: Reads and analyzes existing code during context gathering since the problem is code-level

Asks the right questions: which provider behaviors actually differ (auth, idempotency, webhook handling, refund semantics), which are accidentally divergent, what's the migration plan for in-flight payments
Does not miss important questions: error-handling unification approach, observability gap, backward compatibility for in-flight transactions, test coverage starting point
Identifies architectural challenges around boundaries, interfaces, and contracts between payment providers
Presents 2-3 distinct refactoring shapes (e.g., strategy pattern, plugin architecture, provider microservices) with trade-offs and recommendation
Stress-tests from multiple lenses: maintainer adding the 4th provider, system under failure (provider outage mid-payment), adjacent systems (refund flow, reconciliation job)
Applies YAGNI: rejects speculative abstraction layers that aren't needed for the three current providers
Runs a self-review / spec-reviewer pass on the design
Pauses for user approval before handoff
anti_expectations: Does not start refactoring or writing code during the brainstorm
Does not embed pseudocode or function bodies in the design document
Does not route this to harness:refactor — structural rework belongs in brainstorm, not tactical refactor
Does not analyze only the happy path while ignoring partial-failure and provider-outage scenarios
Does not duplicate the same architectural insight across Key Insights, Architectural Decisions, and Risks
Does not propose a generic 'plugin framework' with knobs for features not yet needed

evals.name: scope-decomposition-triggered
evals.prompt: Brainstorm a complete newsletter system for our platform. We need subscriber signup with double opt-in, a daily send pipeline with unsubscribe handling, and analytics on bounces and opens.
evals.expected_output: Flags multi-subsystem scope, proposes decomposition into independent sub-projects, confirms with the user, then brainstorms the first sub-project alone
expectations: Flags that the request spans multiple independent subsystems (subscribe, delivery, analytics) before deep-diving
Proposes decomposition into sub-projects that can each ship value independently
Names the contracts between sub-projects (e.g., subscribers table state shape) as deliberate design artifacts
Confirms decomposition with the user via AskUserQuestion before continuing
After confirmation, brainstorms only the first sub-project — does not bundle all three into one design
anti_expectations: Does not produce a single design document covering subscribe, delivery, and analytics in one artifact
Does not over-split on prompts where sub-pieces share most of their files and ship together
Does not skip the decomposition check on prompts whose titles contain 'system', 'platform', or 'overhaul'

evals.name: visual-companion-on-ui-work
evals.prompt: Brainstorm a redesign of our analytics dashboard. We want to compare layout options — a card grid vs a single-column scroll vs a left-nav-with-detail-pane.
evals.expected_output: Offers the visual companion as a standalone message because the work involves comparing visual layouts, then proceeds with text questions for non-visual decisions
expectations: Offers the visual companion because the work involves comparing visual layouts
Sends the visual companion offer as its own standalone message — no clarifying questions, context, or other content in the same message
Uses AskUserQuestion for subsequent clarifying questions
Produces a design document at docs/plans/ with the chosen layout direction
anti_expectations: Does not combine the visual companion offer with clarifying questions or context in the same message
Does not offer the visual companion on purely backend work where no visual artifacts exist
Does not force every question through the browser after the companion is accepted — text questions stay in the terminal

evals.name: auto-bypass-review-gate
evals.prompt: Run in auto mode — don't wait for approval. Brainstorm adding rate limiting to our public API endpoints.
evals.expected_output: Completes context gathering, exploration, design, and self-review, but bypasses the user approval gate per the prompt instruction
expectations: Detects the bypass instruction in the prompt ('don't wait for approval' / '--auto' / 'no review gate')
Still produces the full design document at docs/plans/
Still runs the self-review / spec-reviewer pass on the design
Skips the user-facing approval pause and flows directly to the next stage
anti_expectations: Does not pause and ask for user approval on the design when bypass is active
Does not skip the spec-reviewer pass just because the user-facing approval is bypassed
Does not skip context gathering, exploration, YAGNI, or other earlier phases — bypass only affects the final user gate

evals.name: out-of-scope-bug-investigation
evals.prompt: Brainstorm why our login flow sometimes returns 500 errors. It happens roughly once every 50 logins and we can't reproduce it locally.
evals.expected_output: Recognizes bug investigation is out of scope and routes the user to direct exploration rather than producing a design doc
expectations: Recognizes the prompt is a bug investigation, not a structural design problem
Tells the user that brainstorm is for structural changes and offers an alternative path (direct exploration, debugging workflow)
Does not force the request through brainstorming phases
anti_expectations: Does not produce a design document for a bug investigation
Does not propose architectural rework as a fix without first investigating the root cause
Does not walk through brainstorming phases for a problem whose output should be a root cause and fix

evals.name: questioning-discipline
evals.prompt: Brainstorm adding webhook delivery to our notification service. The service lives in src/notifications/ and already has an email channel and an in-app channel; channel dispatch is in src/notifications/dispatcher.ts. I want third parties to receive events too.
evals.expected_output: A brainstorm session where codebase-answerable questions (existing channel interface, dispatcher shape, retry behavior) are answered by exploring src/notifications/ instead of asking, every question to the user carries a recommended answer, dependent decisions are asked one at a time in dependency order, and questioning ends when the decision branches are resolved.
expectations: Checks the context layer (.harness/knowledge/context/ — DECISIONS.md D-* ids, PACKAGE.md, GLOSSARY.md) before asking the user or grepping code, and does not re-ask anything a D-* decision already records
Explores src/notifications/ to answer questions about the existing channel interface and dispatcher instead of asking the user
Every AskUserQuestion includes a recommended option listed first and labeled '(Recommended)'
Questions whose answers depend on an earlier unresolved decision are asked one at a time, after the upstream decision is resolved
Batches multiple questions in one AskUserQuestion call only for independent context facts
Questioning stops when identified decision branches are resolved or explicitly parked in Open Questions
anti_expectations: Does not ask the user about facts discoverable in the codebase (existing channel interface, dispatch flow)
Does not ask open-ended questions without offering a recommended answer
Does not dump a long flat list of questions in a single message regardless of dependencies

evals.name: grill-mode-existing-plan
evals.prompt: Grill me on this plan: I want to move our session storage from Postgres to Redis. Plan: (1) add a Redis client wrapper, (2) dual-write sessions to both stores for a week, (3) switch reads to Redis behind a flag, (4) drop the Postgres table. Sessions are read on every authenticated request.
evals.expected_output: An interrogation of the user's plan — walking its decision branches one-by-one (TTL/eviction policy, dual-write failure semantics, flag rollback, data migration for live sessions, Redis durability), one question at a time with recommendations, verifying codebase claims by exploration, then stress-testing the plan and capturing the agreed result as a design doc — without proposing a competing from-scratch design.
expectations: Recognizes grill mode: interrogates the user's existing plan instead of re-deriving a design from scratch
Walks the plan's decision branches one-by-one in dependency order, one question at a time
Each question includes the assistant's recommended answer
Explores the codebase to verify the plan's claims where possible instead of asking
Runs stress-test lenses against the user's plan (failure modes, load, adjacent systems)
Captures the agreed result as a design doc and presents the user review gate
anti_expectations: Does not ignore the user's plan and present 2-3 alternative approaches as if greenfield
Does not ask multiple dependent questions in a single batch
Does not skip the stress test because the plan came from the user
